steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage establishes that King's Cross suggests to Margaret "infinity" and "eternal adventure" — values explicitly opposed to materialism and capitalist London. The correct choice must reflect that idealist, non-material significance without misreading the station as a symbol of capitalism or disillusionment.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'As a point of connection...intangible abundance...': frames King's Cross as a gateway to the countryside's non-material values — infinity, authentic engagement — directly matching the passage's idealist reading. ✓
  • Choice B — 'emblematizes the intrusion of...materialism...into rural spaces': inverts the passage's logic. King's Cross is not associated with materialism; it points away from London's capitalism toward the countryside. ✗
  • Choice C — 'mirrors Margaret's disillusionment...': introduces disillusionment, which contradicts "infinity" and "eternal adventure" — the station inspires wonder, not disappointment. ✗
  • Choice D — 'contrast with St. Pancras suggests...pragmatic capitalistic outlook': neither the passage's comparison with St. Pancras nor capitalism as Margaret's takeaway is supported — this misreads the aesthetic plainness as a capitalist signal. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
